In the 148s-152s sequence, no explicit numeric evaluation is visible. The clock readings reported from the frames are right clock 04:42 at 148s-152s while the left clock decreases from 04:08 to 04:05. In the 168s-172s sequence, no explicit numeric evaluation is visible and the reported readings are left 03:54 and right 03:30. The same evidence supports mapping Black to the right side of the chess clock.

In the 178s-182s sequence, the reported clock readings are stable at left 03:58 and right 03:24 across all five frames. This evidence also states that no visible evaluation number is seen in those frames and is therefore insufficient by itself to identify the first '>4' moment.

The queried event 'earliest moment in the chess game where the on-screen evaluation or advantage indicator shows Black strictly greater than 4 points' appears in several intervals: 45.00s-52.00s, 88.00s-90.00s, 156.00s-161.00s, 209.00s-212.00s, and 240.00s-242.00s. Chronologically, the earliest returned qualifying interval is 45.00s-52.00s.

inference_steps.text: Chronologically, the inspected older frame groups are 148-152s, then 168-172s, then 178-182s, but those windows were previously insufficient because no visible numeric evaluation was grounded there.

The new grounding directly identifies intervals where Black's on-screen evaluation is strictly greater than 4 points: 45-52s, 88-90s, 156-161s, 209-212s, and 240-242s. The earliest qualifying interval among these is 45-52s.
The prior supported side mapping is still that Black corresponds to the right side of the chess clock; the new evidence does not contradict that mapping.

However, the new evidence only grounds the earliest interval where Black's evaluation is over 4; it does not provide a readable clock value from that same earliest 45-52s interval. The readable clock values we have (04:42, 03:30, 03:24) come from later windows, so they cannot be used as the clock reading at the first qualifying moment.
Because the answer requires Black's clock reading at the first over-4 moment, and that same-moment clock reading is not grounded in the evidence provided, no option can be selected reliably.
